title: Meet the Discriminator
concept: The discriminator is a second neural network — essentially a binary classifier — that looks at an image and decides whether it's real (from the training data) or fake (produced by the generator).

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does the generator take as its input?

options.text: A written description of a scene

exercises.explain: Right — the generator always starts from random noise, never from real data.

cards.title: Real or fake?
cards.body: Picture someone handed a stack of photos and asked one simple question about each: is this real, or is it fake? That's the entire job of the network you're about to meet.

cards.title: What is the discriminator?
cards.body: The discriminator is a neural network that takes an image as input and outputs a single decision: is this image real (from the actual training data) or fake (produced by the generator)?

cards.title: The bouncer checking IDs
cards.body: Think of the discriminator like a bouncer at a club entrance, examining every ID that comes through the door. A real driver's license? Let it in. A sloppy forgery with the wrong font? Turn it away. The bouncer only ever makes one call: real or fake — there's no third option.

cards.title: One image, one verdict
cards.body: Show the discriminator a real photo of a dog: it should output something close to 1 (real). Show it one of the generator's early, blurry attempts: it should output something close to 0 (fake).

cards.title: It's not identifying WHAT is in the image
cards.body: You might think the discriminator labels images like "dog" or "cat," the way Unit 132's classifiers did. It doesn't care about content at all — only authenticity. Its one and only question is real vs. fake.

cards.title: Two flavors of classifier
cards.body: How the discriminator's job compares to what you already learned in Unit 132:

right.text:
Any image in
Real or fake out

exercises.prompt: What kind of decision does the discriminator make about an image?

options.text: Which of 1,000 object categories it belongs to

exercises.explain: Right — the discriminator is a binary classifier with exactly two possible answers.

exercises.prompt: True or false: the discriminator is essentially the same type of binary classifier you studied in Unit 132, just aimed at a new question (real vs. fake instead of a specific label).
exercises.explain: True — it's still a binary classifier under the hood. The question it answers has just changed.

exercises.sentence: The discriminator looks at an image and decides whether it's ___ or fake.
exercises.explain: Real vs. fake is the discriminator's only job — nothing about size, blur, or content categories.

exercises.prompt: Once trained, what output would the discriminator give for a real, high-quality training photo?
exercises.explain: A well-trained discriminator pushes its score toward 1 for genuine training images.

exercises.prompt: Your discriminator is shown two images back to back: an untouched photo from your real training set, and a rough, blurry image fresh out of an untrained generator. What should a well-functioning discriminator predict for each, and why does it only ever need ONE type of decision rather than picking from many categories?

options.text: Close to 1 ("real") for the training photo, close to 0 ("fake") for the generator's output — because unlike Unit 132's classifiers, the discriminator only ever answers one binary question: real or fake
Both images should score close to 1, since the discriminator's job is to be encouraging
It should output the object category shown in each image
It should output the exact pixel values of both images

exercises.explain: Exactly! The discriminator is a binary classifier through and through. Next: put these two networks head-to-head and see how they actually relate to each other.
